content:
# Deuteronomy 10 — Disagreement Log
**Chapter:** Deuteronomy 10 (22 verses) | **Arbiter:** SOPHIA

---

## V.1 — "Hew" vs. "Cut" vs. "Carve"

- **EMET:** "Hew for yourself two tablets of stone"
- **LOGOS:** "Cut for yourself two tablets of stone"
- **PNEUMA:** "Carve two stone tablets"
- **Ruling:** "Carve for yourself two tablets of stone" — Hebrew pesal = to hew/carve stone. "Carve" is the most natural English for stonework. LOGOS's "Cut" is less precise; EMET's "Hew" is accurate but slightly archaic.

---

## V.2 — "smashed" vs. "broke" vs. "shattered"

- **EMET:** "the tablets...which you smashed"
- **LOGOS:** "the tablets, which you broke"
- **PNEUMA:** "the tablets, which you shattered"
- **Ruling:** "the tablets, which you shattered" — consistent with ch.9 ruling; Hebrew shavar = to break/shatter. "Shattered" best captures the violent force.

---

## V.4 — "Ten Words" vs. "Ten Commandments"

- **EMET:** "the Ten Words [Aseret ha-Devarim]"
- **LOGOS:** "the Ten Commandments"
- **PNEUMA:** "the Ten Commandments"
- **Ruling:** "the Ten Commandments" — majority (LOGOS/PNEUMA) and established English convention. Hebrew aseret ha-devarim = "the ten words/utterances" — noted in textual notes.

---

## V.4 — "spoke to you" vs. "had spoken to you" vs. "proclaimed to you"

- **EMET:** "which the LORD spoke with you on the mountain"
- **LOGOS:** "that the LORD had spoken to you on the mountain"
- **PNEUMA:** "that the LORD had proclaimed to you on the mountain"
- **Ruling:** "that the LORD had spoken to you on the mountain" — LOGOS's past perfect appropriately marks the prior event. PNEUMA's "proclaimed" is interpretive; "spoken" is literal.

---

## V.6 — "sons of Israel" vs. "people of Israel" vs. "Israelites"

- **EMET:** "the sons of Yisra'el journeyed"
- **LOGOS:** "The people of Israel journeyed"
- **PNEUMA:** "The Israelites journeyed"
- **Ruling:** "the sons of Israel journeyed" — project convention: bney yisrael = "sons of Israel."

---

## V.7 — "wadis" vs. "brooks" vs. "streams" of water

- **EMET:** "a land of wadis of water"
- **LOGOS:** "a land with brooks of water"
- **PNEUMA:** "a land with streams of water"
- **Ruling:** "a land with streams of water" — consistent with ch.8 ruling. "Streams" preferred.

---

## V.8 — "to this day" vs. "unto this day" vs. "as they still do today"

- **EMET:** "unto this day"
- **LOGOS:** "to this day"
- **PNEUMA:** "as they still do today"
- **Ruling:** "to this day" — LOGOS's rendering is the most natural English. PNEUMA's "as they still do today" is a paraphrase.

---

## V.9 — "as the LORD your God spoke to him" vs. "said to him" vs. "told them"

- **EMET:** "as the LORD your God spoke to him"
- **LOGOS:** "as the LORD your God said to him"
- **PNEUMA:** "as the LORD your God told them"
- **Ruling:** "as the LORD your God spoke to him" — Hebrew dibber lo = spoke to him (singular). PNEUMA's "them" changes the number.

---

## V.10 — first-person emphasis

- **EMET:** "And I — I stayed on the mountain as the first days"
- **LOGOS:** "I myself stayed on the mountain, as at the first time"
- **PNEUMA:** "As for me — I stayed on the mountain as I had before"
- **Ruling:** "I myself stayed on the mountain, as at the first time" — LOGOS captures the Hebrew emphatic pronoun wa'anoki with "I myself." PNEUMA's "As for me" is also good but slightly more dramatic than the Hebrew requires.

---

## V.11 — "go for the journey before" vs. "go on your journey before" vs. "lead the people on their journey"

- **EMET:** "Arise, go for the journey before the people"
- **LOGOS:** "Arise, go on your journey before the people"
- **PNEUMA:** "Get up, lead the people on their journey"
- **Ruling:** "Arise, go before the people on their journey" — combines the literal "Arise" (EMET/LOGOS) with the natural "before the people on their journey." PNEUMA's "Get up" is too colloquial for this solemn divine command.

---

## V.12 — "fear" vs. "hold in reverent awe"

- **EMET:** "but to fear the LORD your God"
- **LOGOS:** "but to fear the LORD your God"
- **PNEUMA:** "Simply this: to hold the LORD your God in reverent awe"
- **Ruling:** "but to fear the LORD your God" — EMET/LOGOS preserve yir'ah literally. PNEUMA's "reverent awe" is interpretive and more verbose. The stark simplicity of "fear" is part of the verse's rhetorical force.

---

## V.12 — "with all your soul" vs. "with all your life"

- **EMET:** "with all your heart and with all your soul"
- **LOGOS:** "with all your heart and with all your soul"
- **PNEUMA:** "with all your heart and with all your life"
- **Ruling:** "with all your heart and with all your soul" — Hebrew nefesh rendered "soul" per Shema convention (cf. Deut. 6:5). PNEUMA's "life" is also valid but "soul" is the established rendering here.

---

## V.14 — "heaven of heavens" vs. "highest heavens"

- **EMET:** "the heavens and the heaven of heavens"
- **LOGOS:** "heaven and the heaven of heavens"
- **PNEUMA:** "the heavens, even the highest heavens"
- **Ruling:** "the heavens and the heaven of heavens" — EMET preserves the Hebrew superlative construction (hashamayim ushemei hashamayim) literally. PNEUMA's "highest heavens" is interpretively accurate but smooths over the Hebrew idiom.

---

## V.15 — "set his heart in love on" vs. "took delight to love them" vs. "chose and loved"

- **EMET:** "Only in your fathers did the LORD take delight to love them"
- **LOGOS:** "Yet the LORD set his heart in love on your fathers"
- **PNEUMA:** "Yet the LORD chose your ancestors and loved them"
- **Ruling:** "Yet the LORD set his heart in love on your fathers" — LOGOS best captures chashaq (to be strongly attached/delight in). EMET's "take delight" is a bit loose; PNEUMA separates choosing and loving.

---

## V.16 — "circumcise the foreskin" vs. "circumcise your hearts"

- **EMET:** "you shall circumcise the foreskin of your heart [mul et orlat levavkhem]"
- **LOGOS:** "Circumcise therefore the foreskin of your heart"
- **PNEUMA:** "Circumcise your hearts, then, and stop being so stubborn"
- **Ruling:** "Circumcise therefore the foreskin of your heart" — LOGOS preserves orlah (foreskin) which PNEUMA drops. The anatomical metaphor is theologically important. "Therefore" connects logically to the preceding doxology.

---

## V.16 — "stiff-necked no more" vs. "stop being so stubborn"

- **EMET:** "your neck you shall stiffen no more"
- **LOGOS:** "be no longer stiff-necked"
- **PNEUMA:** "stop being so stubborn"
- **Ruling:** "be no longer stiff-necked" — LOGOS preserves the anatomical metaphor. PNEUMA's "stubborn" is an interpretation. The Hebrew oref (neck) should remain.

---

## V.17 — "does not lift up faces" vs. "is not partial" vs. "shows no partiality"

- **EMET:** "who does not lift up faces"
- **LOGOS:** "who is not partial"
- **PNEUMA:** "who shows no partiality"
- **Ruling:** "who shows no partiality" — "shows no partiality" (PNEUMA/LOGOS combined) is the most natural English idiom for lo yissa panim. EMET's literal "does not lift up faces" is noted in textual notes.

---

## V.18 — "bread and garment" vs. "food and clothing"

- **EMET:** "giving him bread and garment"
- **LOGOS:** "giving him food and clothing"
- **PNEUMA:** "giving them food and clothing"
- **Ruling:** "giving him food and clothing" — LOGOS's "food" (lechem = bread/food) and "clothing" are natural English. EMET's "bread and garment" is more literal but "food and clothing" captures the range of basic provision. PNEUMA's "them" (plural) is inconsistent; the Hebrew is singular (lo).

---

## V.20 — "cling" vs. "hold fast"

- **EMET:** "and to him you shall cling"
- **LOGOS:** "and hold fast to him"
- **PNEUMA:** "Cling to him"
- **Ruling:** "and to him you shall cling" — "cling" (EMET/PNEUMA) preserves the Hebrew dabaq and its relational resonance (Gen. 2:24). LOGOS's "hold fast" is more abstract.

---

## V.21 — "your praise" vs. "your glory"

- **EMET:** "He is your praise [tehillatekha]"
- **LOGOS:** "He is your praise"
- **PNEUMA:** "He is your glory"
- **Ruling:** "He is your praise" — Hebrew tehillatekha = your praise/song of praise. PNEUMA's "glory" (kavod) is a different Hebrew concept.

---

## V.22 — "stars of the heavens" vs. "stars of heaven" vs. "stars in the sky"

- **EMET:** "as the stars of the heavens in multitude"
- **LOGOS:** "as the stars of heaven in multitude"
- **PNEUMA:** "as numerous as the stars in the sky"
- **Ruling:** "as numerous as the stars of the heavens" — Hebrew kekokavei hashamayim. EMET's "in multitude" is redundant when "as numerous as" is used. PNEUMA's "sky" for hashamayim is acceptable but "heavens" is the standard rendering.

---

**Total disagreements logged: 22**

— SOPHIA
